revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q795080]] Wikidata name: Ugajin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese mythology deity (kami)}} [[File:Ugajin_masculine_form.jpg|thumb|Ugajin (宇賀神), masculine form.]]
{{Nihongo|'''Ugajin'''|宇賀神}} is a [[:en:Harvest|Harvest]] and fertility ''[[kami]]'' of [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="watsky233">Watsky, Andrew Mark. (2004). {{Google books|m7okz1DQslwC|''Chikubushima: Deploying the Sacred Arts in Momoyama Japan''|page=233}}</ref><ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA126 ''Handbook of Japanese mythology'' by Michael Ashkenazi p. 126]</ref> Ugajin is represented both as a male and a female, and is often depicted with the body of a snake and the head of a bearded man, for the masculine variant,<ref name="watsky233"/> or the head of a woman, for the female variant. In [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] Buddhism Ugajin was syncretically fused with Buddhist goddess [[Benzaiten]], which became known as Uga Benzaiten or Uga Benten.<ref>{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011}}</ref> The goddess sometimes carries on her head Ugajin's effigy.
In this limited sense, the ''kami'' is part of the Japanese Buddhist pantheon.__NOTOC__
==Gallery== <gallery widths="160px" heights="200px"> File:Ugajin_feminine_form.jpg|Ugajin's feminine form File:Hogonji13s3200.jpg|Statue of Benzaiten, a ''[[torii]]'' and a male Ugajin visible on her head (whose coiled serpent body is barely visible behind her crown) File:Zeniarai Benzaiten Kamakura Snake.jpg|Wooden snake at [[:en:Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine]] </gallery>
== See also == * {{Portal inline|Japan}} * {{Portal inline|Religion}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{Commonscatinline}}

In [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] Buddhism Ugajin was syncretically fused with Buddhist goddess [[Benzaiten]], which became known as Uga Benzaiten or Uga Benten.<ref>{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011}}</ref> The goddess sometimes carries on her head Ugajin's effigy.
In this limited sense, the ''kami'' is part of the Japanese Buddhist pantheon.__NOTOC__
==Gallery== <gallery widths="160px" heights="200px"> File:Ugajin_feminine_form.jpg|Ugajin's feminine form File:Hogonji13s3200.jpg|Statue of Benzaiten, a ''[[torii]]'' and a male Ugajin visible on her head (whose coiled serpent body is barely visible behind her crown) File:Zeniarai Benzaiten Kamakura Snake.jpg|Wooden snake at [[:en:Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine]] </gallery>

{{Nihongo|'''Ugajin'''|宇賀神}} is a [[:en:Harvest|Harvest]] and fertility ''[[kami]]'' of [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="watsky233">Watsky, Andrew Mark. (2004). {{Google books|m7okz1DQslwC|''Chikubushima: Deploying the Sacred Arts in Momoyama Japan''|page=233}}</ref><ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA126 ''Handbook of Japanese mythology'' by Michael Ashkenazi p. 126]</ref> Ugajin is represented both as a male and a female, and is often depicted with the body of a snake and the head of a bearded man, for the masculine variant,<ref name="watsky233"/> or the head of a woman, for the female variant. In [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] Buddhism Ugajin was syncretically fused with Buddhist goddess [[Benzaiten]], which became known as Uga Benzaiten or Uga Benten.<ref>{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011}}</ref> The goddess sometimes carries on her head Ugajin's effigy.
In this limited sense, the ''kami'' is part of the Japanese Buddhist pantheon.__NOTOC__

{{Nihongo|'''Ugajin'''|宇賀神}} is a [[:en:Harvest|Harvest]] and fertility ''[[kami]]'' of [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="watsky233">Watsky, Andrew Mark. (2004). {{Google books|m7okz1DQslwC|''Chikubushima: Deploying the Sacred Arts in Momoyama Japan''|page=233}}</ref><ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA126 ''Handbook of Japanese mythology'' by Michael Ashkenazi p. 126]</ref> Ugajin is represented both as a male and a female, and is often depicted with the body of a snake and the head of a bearded man, for the masculine variant,<ref name="watsky233"/> or the head of a woman, for the female variant. In [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] Buddhism Ugajin was syncretically fused with Buddhist goddess [[Benzaiten]], which became known as Uga Benzaiten or Uga Benten.<ref>{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011}}</ref> The goddess sometimes carries on her head Ugajin's effigy.
In this limited sense, the ''kami'' is part of the Japanese Buddhist pantheon.__NOTOC__

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q795080]] Wikidata name: Ugajin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese mythology deity (kami)}} [[File:Ugajin_masculine_form.jpg|thumb|Ugajin (宇賀神), masculine form.]]
{{Nihongo|'''Ugajin'''|宇賀神}} is a {{ill|Harvest|en|Harvest|WD=Q213753|lt=Harvest}} and fertility ''[[kami]]'' of [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="watsky233">Watsky, Andrew Mark. (2004). {{Google books|m7okz1DQslwC|''Chikubushima: Deploying the Sacred Arts in Momoyama Japan''|page=233}}</ref><ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA126 ''Handbook of Japanese mythology'' by Michael Ashkenazi p. 126]</ref> Ugajin is represented both as a male and a female, and is often depicted with the body of a snake and the head of a bearded man, for the masculine variant,<ref name="watsky233"/> or the head of a woman, for the female variant. In {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} Buddhism Ugajin was syncretically fused with Buddhist goddess [[Benzaiten]], which became known as Uga Benzaiten or Uga Benten.<ref>{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011}}</ref> The goddess sometimes carries on her head Ugajin's effigy.
In this limited sense, the ''kami'' is part of the Japanese Buddhist pantheon.__NOTOC__
==Gallery== <gallery widths="160px" heights="200px"> File:Ugajin_feminine_form.jpg|Ugajin's feminine form File:Hogonji13s3200.jpg|Statue of Benzaiten, a ''[[torii]]'' and a male Ugajin visible on her head (whose coiled serpent body is barely visible behind her crown) File:Zeniarai Benzaiten Kamakura Snake.jpg|Wooden snake at {{ill|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|en|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|WD=Q642681|lt=Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine}} </gallery>
== See also == * {{Portal inline|Japan}} * {{Portal inline|Religion}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ugajin]][[en:Ugajin]][[fr:Ugajin]][[ja:宇賀神]][[ko:우가진]][[th:อูงาจิง]][[zh:宇賀神]] {{Infobox deity}} {{Short description|Japanese mythology deity (kami)}} [[File:Ugajin_masculine_form.jpg|thumb|Ugajin (宇賀神), masculine form.]]
{{Nihongo|'''Ugajin'''|宇賀神}} is a {{ill|Harvest|en|Harvest|WD=Q213753|lt=Harvest|1=Harvest}} and fertility ''[[kami]]'' of [[Japanese mythology]].<ref name="watsky233">Watsky, Andrew Mark. (2004). {{Google books|m7okz1DQslwC|''Chikubushima: Deploying the Sacred Arts in Momoyama Japan''|page=233}}</ref><ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA126 ''Handbook of Japanese mythology'' by Michael Ashkenazi p. 126]</ref> Ugajin is represented both as a male and a female, and is often depicted with the body of a snake and the head of a bearded man, for the masculine variant,<ref name="watsky233"/> or the head of a woman, for the female variant. In {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} Buddhism Ugajin was syncretically fused with Buddhist goddess [[Benzaiten]], which became known as Uga Benzaiten or Uga Benten.<ref>{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011}}</ref> The goddess sometimes carries on her head Ugajin's effigy.
In this limited sense, the ''kami'' is part of the Japanese Buddhist pantheon.__NOTOC__
==Gallery== <gallery widths="160px" heights="200px"> File:Ugajin_feminine_form.jpg|Ugajin's feminine form File:Hogonji13s3200.jpg|Statue of Benzaiten, a ''[[torii]]'' and a male Ugajin visible on her head (whose coiled serpent body is barely visible behind her crown) File:Zeniarai Benzaiten Kamakura Snake.jpg|Wooden snake at {{ill|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|en|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|WD=Q642681|lt=Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|1=Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine}} </gallery>

(2004). {{Google books|m7okz1DQslwC|''Chikubushima: Deploying the Sacred Arts in Momoyama Japan''|page=233}}</ref><ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=gqs-y9R2AekC&pg=PA126 ''Handbook of Japanese mythology'' by Michael Ashkenazi p. 126]</ref> Ugajin is represented both as a male and a female, and is often depicted with the body of a snake and the head of a bearded man, for the masculine variant,<ref name="watsky233"/> or the head of a woman, for the female variant.
